title: "Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they will be filled."
scripture_ref:
Matthew 5:1-12 The Beatitudes Part 3
‘’Now when Jesus saw the crowds, he went up on a mountainside and sat down. His disciples came to him, and he began to teach them.
scripture_text: He said: “Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are those who mourn, for they will be comforted. Blessed are the meek for they will inherit the earth. Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they will be filled. Blessed are the merciful, for they will be shown mercy. Blessed are the pure in heart, for they will see God. Blessed are the peacemakers, for they will be called children of God. Blessed are those who are persecuted because of righteousness, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are you when people insult you, persecute you and falsely say all kinds of evil against you because of me. Rejoice and be glad, because great is your reward in heaven, for in the same way they persecuted the prophets who were before you.’’
devotional:
The Pharisees did not feel a need to seek for righteousness; they viewed themselves as being righteous.

This beatitude describes what should be in the heart of those who are living in the kingdom of God. There should be an overwhelming desire for righteousness. We need to cultivate a love for righteousness. The thirst and hunger mentioned here is an unquenchable one, the result of prolonged deprivation. The Puritan Thomas Watson wrote, "He who has most need of righteousness is he that least wants it."

Why is it important that we develop and encourage such a hunger in our own hearts and in the hearts of others? Righteousness brings hope and security to a person. Why? For righteousness brings satisfaction. The Greek word for filled is chortazo, which was often used to describe the feeding of animals until they were so full they could eat no more. When we lack contentment and satisfaction we will turn to sin in order to find fulfilment. If a person has a strong desire and appetite for righteousness, it will effect how they treat others? Fulfilment in this life can only be experienced as we increase in righteousness.

If we were to divide the beatitudes into two groups of four, the first four speak of those who feel their need of righteousness, while the second four is speaking about those who display righteousness. The first four are speaking about the internal attitudes, while the second four are speaking about external relationships, relationship with God and people.

"Blessed are the merciful for they shall be shown mercy."

The Pharisees were incapable of showing mercy. What is mercy? The Greek word here was originally used to describe the emotion aroused by contact with an undeserved affliction that comes on someone else. We might use the words "sympathy," "pity" or "compassion." Mercy may involve such acts as pardon, forgiveness, or the mitigation of penalties. Mercy is experienced and exercised by a person who has another person in his power, or under his authority, or from whom no kindness can be claimed.

Mercy is an essential requirement for healthy relationships. Having a heart full of mercy will help a person respond properly to the pain that comes through difficult relationships, and will enable a person to reach out to those who are less fortunate.

A person, who is incapable of showing mercy, is dehumanised, for mercy is one of the noblest expressions of what it means to be human and made in the likeness of God. The person who is poor in spirit, mourning over sin, meek, submissive to God in all things, and hungering and thirsting after righteousness is going to be merciful. If a person is not merciful, then the other qualities are also lacking.
prayer: Prayer, Father, may my life display mercy to others
html:
"Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they will be filled."

Matthew 5:1-12 The Beatitudes Part 3 ‘’Now when Jesus saw the crowds, he went up on a mountainside and sat down. His disciples came to him, and he began to teach them.
He said: “Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are those who mourn, for they will be comforted. Blessed are the meek for they will inherit the earth. Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they will be filled. Blessed are the merciful, for they will be shown mercy. Blessed are the pure in heart, for they will see God. Blessed are the peacemakers, for they will be called children of God. Blessed are those who are persecuted because of righteousness, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are you when people insult you, persecute you and falsely say all kinds of evil against you because of me. Rejoice and be glad, because great is your reward in heaven, for in the same way they persecuted the prophets who were before you.’’

The Pharisees did not feel a need to seek for righteousness; they viewed themselves as being righteous.

This beatitude describes what should be in the heart of those who are living in the kingdom of God. There should be an overwhelming desire for righteousness. We need to cultivate a love for righteousness. The thirst and hunger mentioned here is an unquenchable one, the result of prolonged deprivation. The Puritan Thomas Watson wrote, "He who has most need of righteousness is he that least wants it."

Why is it important that we develop and encourage such a hunger in our own hearts and in the hearts of others? Righteousness brings hope and security to a person. Why? For righteousness brings satisfaction. The Greek word for filled is chortazo, which was often used to describe the feeding of animals until they were so full they could eat no more. When we lack contentment and satisfaction we will turn to sin in order to find fulfilment. If a person has a strong desire and appetite for righteousness, it will effect how they treat others? Fulfilment in this life can only be experienced as we increase in righteousness.

If we were to divide the beatitudes into two groups of four, the first four speak of those who feel their need of righteousness, while the second four is speaking about those who display righteousness. The first four are speaking about the internal attitudes, while the second four are speaking about external relationships, relationship with God and people.

"Blessed are the merciful for they shall be shown mercy."

The Pharisees were incapable of showing mercy. What is mercy? The Greek word here was originally used to describe the emotion aroused by contact with an undeserved affliction that comes on someone else. We might use the words "sympathy," "pity" or "compassion." Mercy may involve such acts as pardon, forgiveness, or the mitigation of penalties. Mercy is experienced and exercised by a person who has another person in his power, or under his authority, or from whom no kindness can be claimed.

Mercy is an essential requirement for healthy relationships. Having a heart full of mercy will help a person respond properly to the pain that comes through difficult relationships, and will enable a person to reach out to those who are less fortunate.

A person, who is incapable of showing mercy, is dehumanised, for mercy is one of the noblest expressions of what it means to be human and made in the likeness of God. The person who is poor in spirit, mourning over sin, meek, submissive to God in all things, and hungering and thirsting after righteousness is going to be merciful. If a person is not merciful, then the other qualities are also lacking.

Prayer: Prayer, Father, may my life display mercy to others
